pick.rationale: Duke enters as the #1 overall seed with a 31-2 record and a historic +39.17 efficiency margin, featuring the nation's top defense and fourth-ranked offense. While the Blue Devils are missing starters Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba II, they dominated the ACC Tournament without them, demonstrating overwhelming depth. Siena's MAAC championship credentials and negative efficiency margin place them approximately 190 ranking spots below Duke, leaving the Saints without a credible statistical or tactical path to mount a historic 16-over-1 upset.

reasoningStep.title: Duke vs Siena 1/16 Statistical and Contextual Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Duke holds an overwhelming statistical advantage as the #1 ranked team nationally with elite metrics on both ends. Siena, as MAAC champions, ranks outside the top 185 and lacks the efficiency or high-major experience to challenge a national title favorite. Duke's injury absences are mitigated by proven depth and ACC Tournament dominance without those players.
evidence: Duke ranked #1 KenPom/Torvik, +39.17 adj EM, #1 defense, #4 offense, 31-2 record
Siena ranked ~187-192, -2.11 adj EM, below-average offense (~197-207), 23-11 MAAC record
Duke missing Caleb Foster (broken foot) and Patrick Ngongba II but won ACC Tournament without them
Siena won MAAC tournament as #3 seed, first bid since 2020, beat Merrimack 64-54 in final
Historical: #16 seeds 2-150 all-time vs #1 seeds, Siena lacks upset indicators

result.text: Key March Madness injuries to know while filling out your men’s NCAA Tournament bracket - The Athletic Premier League Champions League 2026 Men's World Cup F1 Tennis FA Cup Cricket La Liga Europa League Women's Football Olympics Global Sports Cycling Sailing Sports Business Boxing Golf Serie A Bundesliga International Football NFL NBA MLB World Baseball Classic NHL MLS Transfer News FPL League Cup Championship League One League Two Women's Euros NWSL College Sports NCAAF NCAAM Peak MMA Motorsports NASCAR Culture Memorabilia & Collectibles Gaming Sports Betting The PL Is Being Left Behind Welcome to Chelsea, Liam Rosenior PL Player of the Year Nominees Log In Subscribe Now Tickets by Viagogo Connections: Sports Edition REGION U.S. International Search Top News Podcasts NCAAM Bracket Scores & Schedule Teams Alabama Arizona Arkansas Duke Florida Gonzaga Houston Illinois Iowa State Kansas Louisville Miami (FL) Michigan State Michigan Nebraska North Carolina Purdue Saint Mary's St. John's Tennessee Texas Tech UConn Vanderbilt Virginia Wisconsin Standings Beat Our Expert's Bracket Tournament Projections Printable Bracket Predictions Game Men's March Madness LIVE First Four Updates Tournament Forecast Challenge Our Experts The Perfect Bracket Bracket Busters NBA Prospects to Watch Key Injuries To Know Key March Madness injuries to know while filling out your men’s NCAA Tournament bracket Patrick Ngongba II and Duke are the overall No. 1 seed, but a couple of injuries could make it more vulnerable. Grant Halverson / Getty Images By Jim Root March 17, 2026 Updated 3:19 pm EDT Share full article 2 The Athletic has live coverage of the First Four from 2026 Men’s March Madness. Roster attrition is a factor during the postseason in any sport. Optimistically, college basketball’s shorter regular season should mean the title picture is less altered by injuries than, say, the NBA. That is not necessarily the case this season, though. The injury bug has recently bitten two No. 1 seeds (Duke and Michigan), and a third (Arizona) played through a flurry of nagging injuries late in the season. Multiple All-Americans on dark-horse title contenders are out for the season. Advertisement Below is a repository of all the biggest injuries impacting the men’s NCAA Tournament. We have split them into two categories: Injuries worth monitoring (the players could or will return at some point during the tournament) and out for the season. Injuries to monitor Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba II, Duke Both Duke starters missed the ACC tournament. The Blue Devils managed to win that event regardless, but they might need some reinforcements to handle the competition they will see if they reach the Elite Eight and beyond. Ngongba, out with a foot injury, is arguably the more valuable piece due to his defensive presence, elite finishing and sneaky-strong passing. Fortunately for the Blue Devils, he is also much closer to returning than Foster, though

result.text: Siena Wins 2026 Air Force Reserve MAAC Men’s Basketball Championship Powered by Northeast Beef; Saints Secure Seventh Title in Program History - Metro Atlantic Athletic Conference Skip To Main Content Metro Atlantic Athletic Conference Countdown Main Navigation Menu Sport Navigation Menu Men's Basketball 3/11/2026 1:47:53 AM Siena Wins 2026 Air Force Reserve MAAC Men’s Basketball Championship Powered by Northeast Beef; Saints Secure Seventh Title in Program History Story Links ATLANTIC CITY, NJ (Mar. 11, 2026) – The Siena Saints won the 2026 Air Force Reserve Metro Atlantic Athletic Conference (MAAC) Men’s Basketball Championship title on Tuesday night at Jim Whelan Boardwalk Hall. No. 3 Siena upended top-seeded Merrimack College, 64-54, winning its first title since 2020 and the program’s seventh overall league crown. No. 3 Siena – 64 | No. 1 Merrimack – 54 Jim Whelan Boardwalk Hall | ATLANTIC CITY, NJ Box Score (XML) Box Score (PDF) The championship began Merrimack going ahead 6-2, but the contest quickly shifted in Siena’s favor, with the Saints taking the lead for good in the game, going on a 13-0, and 18-3 run for a 5:41 stretch, hitting its final five straight field goals by the second media timeout. Merrimack went on a 5:43 scoreless drought and was 1-for-11 during the timeframe. Additionally, a 9-0 run for 3:23 added to the Saints rally, extending their lead to 22-5. At the final media of the half (3:41), Merrimack scored five straight points to cut the game to single digits (28-20), hitting 4-of-5 field goals, while Siena was scoreless for 2:25. With 3:03 left and Siena up 13, the Warriors closed out the half with a 10-0 spurt, which cut the score to a single possession (33-30). The 10-0 run was highlighted by back-to-back triples from Andres Marrero and Ernest Shelton. Siena’s largest lead of the half was 15 points, coming at the 9:32 mark. At the start of the second half, Siena quickly scored the first eight points, with Gavin Doty accounting for six of the points behind a pair of field goals and two free throws. Merrimack was held scoreless by the first media break at 15:53, with Siena leading 41-30. Siena’s scoring streak ascended to a 12-0 run by the 11:41 mark, putting them ahead 45-30. After nine minutes of being held scoreless the Warriors first basket in half was a layup by Kevair Kennedy at 10:58. With just under eight minutes on the game clock, Siena led 47-32, as each team fell into a slump. Merrimack was scoreless for 3:04 and 0-of-4 following its only made field goal of the half to that point, while Siena’s drought was 2:32 and they were also 0-of-4 (1 of last 8). The drought eventually ended for each side, and Merrimack brought the contest back to single digits following a 7-0 and 10-2 run with back-to-back threes by Todd Brogna and four points by Kennedy to cut the game to 49-40. By the final media break, Siena led 51-45. Out of the media timeout, a pair of free throws by Kennedy capped off 12-2 rally over a 2:45 span for

Roster attrition is a factor during the postseason in any sport. Optimistically, college basketball’s shorter regular season should mean the title picture is less altered by injuries than, say, the NBA. That is not necessarily the case this season, though. The injury bug has recently bitten two No. 1 seeds (Duke and Michigan), and a third (Arizona) played through a flurry of nagging injuries late in the season. Multiple All-Americans on dark-horse title contenders are out for the season. Advertisement Below is a repository of all the biggest injuries impacting the men’s NCAA Tournament. We have split them into two categories: Injuries worth monitoring (the players could or will return at some point during the tournament) and out for the season. Injuries to monitor Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba II, Duke Both Duke starters missed the ACC tournament. The Blue Devils managed to win that event regardless, but they might need some reinforcements to handle the competition they will see if they reach the Elite Eight and beyond. Ngongba, out with a foot injury, is arguably the more valuable piece due to his defensive presence, elite finishing and sneaky-strong passing. Fortunately for the Blue Devils, he is also much closer to returning than Foster, though
